items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).
Correct answer: surroundings warm up. “surroundings cool” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Combustion of methane. “Thermal decomposition of limestone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If bonds broken cost 1000 kJ/mol and bonds made release 1300 kJ/mol, what is ΔH (kJ/mol)?
items.why: The real figure is -300 kJ/mol. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
